news_summary: Advancements in neurostimulation, Alzheimer’s treatment, cellular health, and heat-resilient healthcare are improving recovery, brain safety, aging, and climate-related health risks.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans les domaines de la neurostimulation, du traitement de la maladie d'Alzheimer, de la santé cellulaire et des soins de santé adaptés aux vagues de chaleur permettent d'améliorer la récupération, la protection du cerveau, le vieillissement et la gestion des risques sanitaires liés au climat.
news_summary_es: Los avances en neuroestimulación, tratamiento del Alzheimer, salud celular y atención sanitaria resistente al calor están mejorando la recuperación, la protección del cerebro, el envejecimiento y los riesgos para la salud relacionados con el clima.

articles.title: Neurostimulation restores feeling in paralysed hand for months after
articles.summary: Keith Thomas, 48, has paralysis, but brain stimulation gave him some feeling and movement in his hands – and this was maintained even when the stimulation was turned off
articles.content:
Keith Thomas, 48, has paralysis, but brain stimulation gave him some feeling and movement in his hands – and this was maintained even when the stimulation was turned off

Keith Thomas has paralysis, but can now move and feel his hands again thanks to a brain implant MATTHEW LIBASSI/Feinstein Institutes for Medical research

A man who became paralysed after a diving accident six years ago regained the ability to move and feel pressure in his hands thanks to brain stimulation. Now, researchers have revealed he maintained this ability for months after the stimulation was turned off. This suggests the intervention has caused a rerouting of his neuronal connections through neuroplasticity.

“We turned everything off completely, for many months, and yet he’s maintained these gains,” says Chad Bouton at the Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research in New York. “That’s unheard of.”

Keith Thomas, 48, was paralysed from the chest down in July 2020. He had no sensation or control over his limbs and had significant muscle wasting, says Bouton.

Advertisement

In 2023, Bouton and his colleagues performed a double neural bypass surgery on Thomas, placing five electrodes into his brain in regions associated with arm movements and feeling. They then connected computer cables to these electrodes, so artificial intelligence could interpret his movement intentions. That information was then wired into electronic splints that stimulated his arms, hands and fingers to carry out his intended movements, enabling him to pick up coffee cups and scratch his face.

To recreate the sense of feeling, the team embedded force sensors into 3D-printed wearable devices for Thomas’s hands and fingers, which sent feedback via electrical stimulation into the brain’s sensory areas.

After conducting a series of experiments – which even involved Thomas feeling objects through another person’s hand – Bouton says the team planned to stop the stimulation for about a month, to test for any lingering effects. “Then we had a fire in the building, and it actually forced us to stop stimulation for even longer than we’d planned, for about three months.”

Thomas has even been able to move and feel sensations through another person’s hand MATTHEW LIBASSI/Feinstein Institutes for Medical research

The unexpected interruption led to surprising findings: Thomas continued to maintain strength, feeling and function in his hands. “He’s now also controlling individual fingers with even more accuracy, so that’s big,” says Bouton.

In a video interview with New Scientist, Thomas raised his elbows nearly to shoulder level and described feeling “tingling” in his wrist in response to pressure, even when he’s “unplugged from the computer”. “When I first felt it, it was amazing,” he says. “I’m used to it now.”

Sergey Stavisky at the University of California, Davis, says the work suggests that this approach promotes lasting recovery of the nervous system. “The goal is to help the nervous system partially heal so the person can move their own body better,” he says.

Free newsletter Sign up to Health Check Sign up to newsletter

“If these improvements persist even when the system is turned off, then the device is doing more than temporarily restoring function,” says Daniel Lu at the University of California, Los Angeles. “It may be helping the nervous system reorganise itself through neuroplasticity.”

This describes the brain’s ability to rewire itself by forming new neural connections, such as after an injury or even in response to a new hobby. “After an injury such as spinal cord injury, those same mechanisms may help restore function by strengthening spared pathways or recruiting alternative circuits, allowing neural signals to travel through networks that were previously too weak to support meaningful movement,” says Lu.

The researchers have observed stronger neural responses in Thomas’s sensory cortex since the intervention.

But this is just a single case report, so it’s unclear how well this approach would work on other people with paralysis from different types of injuries. Charles Greenspon at the University of Chicago says he has spent years working on stimulation to restore touch in people with spinal cord injuries and continues to find that some respond better than others, and some not at all. “And we have no idea why,” he says. “So, the question is: can you replicate it? This is a really ambitious study, but we need to see them replicating their results in more participants before we believe the hype.”

As to Thomas’s future, “at this point now we know nothing’s impossible, or anything’s possible”, says Boulton. “I think it’s possible he will continue to to improve,” he adds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where brain stimulation enabled a man with paralysis to regain lasting sensation and movement in his hands, demonstrating neuroplasticity and potential for broader application in spinal cord injury treatment. The impact is meaningful for many people with paralysis, and the article provides detailed context on the intervention and its implications.
articles.personality_title: Brain stimulation helps paralyzed man regain hand movement and feeling for months
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Keith Thomas, 48, became paralyzed from the chest down after a diving accident. He lost all feeling and control in his hands and arms.

**What happened** – In 2023, doctors implanted electrodes in Keith’s brain and connected them to a computer system. This system read his thoughts about moving his hands and sent signals to special devices that helped his hands move and feel pressure. Unexpectedly, even after the stimulation was turned off for months due to a fire, Keith kept the ability to move and feel with his hands.

**Impact** – This is unusual because brain stimulation usually works only while it is on. Keith’s lasting improvement shows his brain may have rewired itself, a process called neuroplasticity. This means his nervous system might be healing and creating new pathways to restore movement and feeling.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to test this method on more people to see if it works for others with paralysis. They will also study how the brain changes to better understand and improve the treatment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Brain stimulation helped a man with paralysis regain hand movement and feeling that lasted even after the treatment stopped, showing the brain’s ability to heal itself.
articles.personality_title_fr: La stimulation cérébrale permet à un homme paralysé de retrouver le mouvement et la sensation des mains pendant des mois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Keith Thomas, 48 ans, est devenu paralysé de la poitrine vers le bas après un accident de plongée. Il avait perdu toute sensation et contrôle de ses mains et bras.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2023, des médecins ont implanté des électrodes dans le cerveau de Keith et les ont reliées à un système informatique. Ce système lisait ses intentions de mouvement et envoyait des signaux à des dispositifs spéciaux qui aidaient ses mains à bouger et à ressentir la pression. De façon inattendue, même après l’arrêt de la stimulation pendant plusieurs mois à cause d’un incendie, Keith a conservé la capacité de bouger et de sentir avec ses mains.

**Impact** – C’est inhabituel car la stimulation cérébrale fonctionne généralement seulement lorsqu’elle est active. L’amélioration durable de Keith montre que son cerveau a peut-être reconfiguré ses connexions, un phénomène appelé neuroplasticité. Cela signifie que son système nerveux pourrait guérir et créer de nouvelles voies pour restaurer le mouvement et la sensation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester cette méthode sur d’autres personnes pour voir si elle fonctionne avec d’autres paralysies. Ils vont aussi étudier comment le cerveau change pour mieux comprendre et améliorer ce traitement.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La stimulation cérébrale a aidé un homme paralysé à retrouver le mouvement et la sensation des mains, même après l’arrêt du traitement, montrant la capacité du cerveau à se réparer.
articles.personality_title_es: La estimulación cerebral ayuda a un hombre paralizado a recuperar el movimiento y la sensación en las manos durante meses
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Keith Thomas, de 48 años, quedó paralizado desde el pecho hacia abajo tras un accidente de buceo. Perdió toda sensación y control en sus manos y brazos.

**Qué pasó** – En 2023, los médicos implantaron electrodos en el cerebro de Keith y los conectaron a un sistema informático. Este sistema leía sus intenciones de movimiento y enviaba señales a dispositivos especiales que ayudaban a sus manos a moverse y sentir presión. Sorprendentemente, incluso después de apagar la estimulación durante meses por un incendio, Keith mantuvo la capacidad de mover y sentir con sus manos.

**Impacto** – Esto es inusual porque la estimulación cerebral suele funcionar solo mientras está activa. La mejora duradera de Keith muestra que su cerebro pudo haberse reorganizado, un proceso llamado neuroplasticidad. Esto significa que su sistema nervioso podría estar sanando y creando nuevas vías para restaurar el movimiento y la sensación.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar este método en más personas para ver si funciona con otras parálisis. También estudiarán cómo cambia el cerebro para entender mejor y mejorar el tratamiento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La estimulación cerebral ayudó a un hombre paralizado a recuperar el movimiento y la sensación en sus manos que duraron incluso después de detener el tratamiento, mostrando la capacidad del cerebro para curarse.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two stylized hands made of interconnected glowing neural pathways, gently reaching toward each other with subtle electric pulses flowing between them, set against a soft, natural background of muted earth tones and warm light that evokes healing and renewed connection.

articles.title: Next-generation Alzheimer’s drug reduces risk of serious brain bleeds
articles.summary: A new kind of antibody drug that more efficiently enters the brain could slow the progression of Alzheimer's disease with a much lower risk of dangerous side effects than existing therapies
articles.content:
A new kind of antibody drug that more efficiently enters the brain could slow the progression of Alzheimer's disease with a much lower risk of dangerous side effects than existing therapies

A coloured MRI scan of someone with Alzheimer’s disease. The different colours represent the direction of water diffusion, revealing the orientation of nerve fibres, which are disrupted in Alzheimer’s MARK AND MARY STEVENS NEUROIMAGING AND INFORMATICS INSTITUTE/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

A drug that clears amyloid from the brain by hijacking its iron-transport system may be a much safer treatment for Alzheimer’s disease than the approved amyloid-targeting therapies. These controversial treatments can cause brain changes that lead to bleeds and swelling in up to 40 per cent of patients, but the new drug’s more efficient entry system allows it to be used at lower doses, with fewer side effects.

Over the past few years, two antibody drugs – lecanemab and donanemab – have been approved for treating early-stage Alzheimer’s disease in the US and the UK. This was the first time therapies have been approved to slow the course of the condition; however, many have argued that their effect on slowing cognitive decline is modest. This, combined with the drugs’ high prices, means they aren’t available on the UK’s national health services.

Trials have also shown that around 20 and 40 per cent of people taking lecanemab and donanemab, respectively, develop amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIAs), where fluid leaks from blood vessels in the brain. In some people, this can cause serious bleeds and seizures. There were even several related deaths in the trials.

Advertisement

The pharmaceutical company Roche has designed another antibody drug called trontinemab. Like with the other therapies, this clears clumps of the protein beta-amyloid – which is widely thought to contribute to Alzheimer’s disease – from the brain.

In the latest development, Luka Kulic at Roche in Basel, Switzerland, and his colleagues have found that trontinemab causes significantly fewer ARIAs than other antibody drugs and more quickly reduces amyloid levels in the brain, with this being maintained for a year at least. The findings were presented at the Alzheimer’s Association International Conference in London on 14 July.

This is probably because trontinemab’s entry into the brain differs from that of other amyloid drugs. It consists of an antibody that binds to a receptor that usually transports iron across the blood-brain barrier, meaning it is shuttled into the brain, rather than having to slowly drift across via diffusion, like with the approved amyloid therapies. In the brain, the drug can then bind to and help clear beta-amyloid.

Free newsletter Sign up to Health Check Sign up to newsletter

As a result, trontinemab crosses the blood-brain barrier much more effectively to reach its target sites. “You don’t need as much of [trontinemab] because it can get to the brain much easier, and therefore it’s less invasive on your brain,” says Luciana Maffei at the University of Oxford. “With the other ones, you have to use a lot of [the drugs] with the hope that just some of it gets through, so it’s quite disruptive.”

Last year, Roche released results showing that around 90 per cent of participants with mild cognitive impairment or mild-to-moderate Alzheimer’s disease went from having excessive amyloid build-up in their brain to normal levels within six months of taking a monthly dose of trontinemab. With other amyloid antibodies, it takes more than a year for around three-quarters of patients to get to this point.

“The reduction in amyloid was remarkable; [trontinemab] achieved massive reductions quicker than we’ve seen before,” says Nick Fox at University College London. Crucially, less than 5 per cent of these participants developed ARIAs.

Although Roche hasn’t released data on how trontinemab affects cognitive decline, the findings so far suggest it probably matches, or even outperforms, lecanemab and donanemab, says Fox. “What all the other studies have told us is the key to clinical benefit is getting amyloid low enough, quickly enough, so my prediction would be that [trontinemab] would do at least as well as the licensed therapies. It could potentially do even better.”

If these greater benefits justify the cost, which is always going to be high with an infused antibody, trontinemab could even become available on the UK’s national health services, says Fox. It may also have the added benefits of requiring fewer infusions and fewer brain scans to check for ARIAs, further driving down the cost, he says.

Roche is now planning two larger trials to assess whether trontinemab slows cognitive decline among people with early-stage Alzheimer’s and if it prevents the condition in people who are particularly at risk.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a new Alzheimer’s drug, trontinemab, that significantly reduces dangerous side effects compared to existing therapies and more rapidly lowers amyloid levels in the brain, potentially slowing disease progression. This represents a meaningful medical breakthrough with broad implications for millions affected by Alzheimer's worldwide. The article provides detailed context on the drug’s mechanism, trial results, and potential impact on treatment accessibility and patient safety.
articles.personality_title: New Alzheimer’s drug lowers brain risks and clears harmful proteins faster
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer’s disease is a brain illness that causes memory loss and thinking problems. Some new medicines help slow it down by removing harmful protein clumps called amyloid, but these medicines can cause serious side effects like brain bleeds.

**What happened** – A company named Roche created a new drug called trontinemab. This drug uses the brain’s natural iron transport system to enter the brain more easily. It clears amyloid protein faster and causes fewer side effects than current medicines. In recent tests, most patients had normal amyloid levels after six months, and less than 5% had brain swelling or bleeds.

**Impact** – Trontinemab’s ability to enter the brain more efficiently means doctors can use lower doses, making it safer. It works faster than existing drugs, which usually take over a year to clear amyloid in most patients. This could make treatment less risky and easier for patients, possibly allowing more people to get help.

**What's next step** – Roche will start bigger studies to see if trontinemab not only clears amyloid but also slows down memory loss and thinking problems in early Alzheimer’s patients. They will also test if it can prevent Alzheimer’s in people who might get it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new Alzheimer’s drug, trontinemab, clears harmful brain proteins faster with fewer side effects, offering hope for safer and better treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau médicament contre Alzheimer réduit les risques cérébraux et élimine plus vite les protéines nocives
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d’Alzheimer est une maladie du cerveau qui provoque des pertes de mémoire et des troubles de la pensée. Certains médicaments récents ralentissent la maladie en éliminant des amas de protéines appelées amyloïde, mais ils peuvent causer des effets secondaires graves comme des saignements cérébraux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise nommée Roche a créé un nouveau médicament appelé trontinemab. Ce médicament utilise le système naturel de transport du fer dans le cerveau pour y entrer plus facilement. Il élimine l’amyloïde plus rapidement et provoque moins d’effets secondaires que les médicaments actuels. Lors des derniers essais, la plupart des patients avaient des niveaux normaux d’amyloïde après six mois, et moins de 5 % ont eu des gonflements ou saignements cérébraux.

**Impact** – La capacité de trontinemab à pénétrer plus efficacement dans le cerveau permet d’utiliser des doses plus faibles, ce qui le rend plus sûr. Il agit plus vite que les médicaments existants, qui mettent souvent plus d’un an pour éliminer l’amyloïde chez la plupart des patients. Cela pourrait rendre le traitement moins risqué et plus facile, permettant à plus de personnes d’en bénéficier.

**Prochaine étape** – Roche va lancer des études plus larges pour vérifier si trontinemab ralentit aussi la perte de mémoire et les troubles de la pensée chez les patients atteints d’Alzheimer au stade précoce. Ils testeront aussi s’il peut prévenir la maladie chez les personnes à risque.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau médicament contre Alzheimer, trontinemab, élimine plus vite les protéines nocives du cerveau avec moins d’effets secondaires, offrant un espoir de traitements plus sûrs et efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo medicamento para Alzheimer reduce riesgos cerebrales y elimina proteínas dañinas más rápido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer es un problema del cerebro que causa pérdida de memoria y dificultades para pensar. Algunos medicamentos nuevos ayudan a frenar la enfermedad eliminando grupos de proteínas llamadas amiloide, pero pueden causar efectos secundarios graves como sangrados cerebrales.

**Qué pasó** – La empresa Roche creó un nuevo medicamento llamado trontinemab. Este medicamento usa el sistema natural de transporte de hierro del cerebro para entrar más fácilmente. Elimina la amiloide más rápido y causa menos efectos secundarios que los medicamentos actuales. En pruebas recientes, la mayoría de los pacientes tenían niveles normales de amiloide después de seis meses, y menos del 5 % tuvo inflamación o sangrados en el cerebro.

**Impacto** – La capacidad de trontinemab para entrar mejor al cerebro permite usar dosis más bajas, haciéndolo más seguro. Funciona más rápido que los medicamentos existentes, que tardan más de un año en eliminar la amiloide en la mayoría de los pacientes. Esto podría hacer que el tratamiento sea menos riesgoso y más fácil para los pacientes, permitiendo que más personas reciban ayuda.

**Próximo paso** – Roche comenzará estudios más grandes para ver si trontinemab no solo elimina la amiloide, sino que también frena la pérdida de memoria y problemas de pensamiento en pacientes con Alzheimer temprano. También probarán si puede prevenir la enfermedad en personas con riesgo.

**Una frase clave** – Un nuevo medicamento para Alzheimer, trontinemab, elimina proteínas dañinas del cerebro más rápido y con menos efectos secundarios, ofreciendo esperanza para tratamientos más seguros y efectivos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized human brain composed of intricate, intertwining golden threads representing healthy nerve fibers, with a gentle, glowing antibody figure shaped like a key unlocking and clearing clusters of soft, translucent blue amyloid plaques, all set against a calm, neutral background symbolizing safe and efficient healing without disruption.

articles.title: Restoring ability to remove zombie cells may keep us sharp as we age
articles.summary: Zombie, or senescent, cells accumulate in our body with age, when we become less able to clear them. But a study in mice suggests that a drug could restore this ability and improve ageing outcomes
articles.content:
Zombie, or senescent, cells accumulate in our body with age, when we become less able to clear them. But a study in mice suggests that a drug could restore this ability and improve ageing outcomes

Transmission electron micrograph of human neutrophils, immune cells that can turn rogue with age ROSSANA MELO/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

As we age, our body loses its ability to clear out zombie-like cells that have stopped dividing, instead dripping out poison and turning other cells rogue. Restoring the ability to remove these “senescent” cells prevented signs of cognitive decline and frailty in older mice, with hints that the same outcomes could occur in people.

“What’s attractive about this study is that it is not trying to kill [senescent] cells directly,” says Derek Gilroy at University College London, who wasn’t involved in the research. “It is repairing the body’s own waste-disposal system that should have removed them in the first place.”

To better understand why this system goes awry, Katrin Andreasson at Stanford University, California, and her colleagues compared mice aged 6 to 8 months (roughly equivalent to people in their 20s) with mice aged 23 to 25 months (equivalent to people in their late 60s or 70s). They found that the older mice had more of these senescent cells in their organs, including the liver and spleen, as well as in their bone marrow.

Advertisement

Specifically, the senescent cells were neutrophils, immune cells that are normally the body’s first line of defence against infection. These would normally be cleared away by macrophages residing in tissue, which remove damaged cells and debris, but this ability declines with age.

Andreasson and her colleagues traced this decline to prostaglandin E2, a signalling molecule that increases with age. They found that higher prostaglandin E2 activity overstimulates a receptor called EP2 on tissue-resident macrophages, which leaves them less able to remove senescent cells.

Free newsletter Sign up to Health Check Sign up to newsletter

Next, the team tested whether blocking this overstimulation could improve signs of ageing by genetically modifying the EP2 gene from tissue-resident macrophages in older mice. These animals went on to clear more senescent neutrophils and showed signs of healthier ageing, including lower levels of inflammation, reduced muscle loss, less visceral fat and better mobility, compared with unmodified mice of the same age. They also performed almost as well as young mice in memory tests. “We were very surprised at the magnitude of the effect,” says Andreasson.

The researchers also tested an experimental drug that blocks EP2. When given orally to older mice for two months, this caused similar age-related improvements to those experienced by the genetically modified mice.

“This is a very promising study, but we need to be careful,” says Gilroy. “EP2 is part of a normal signalling system, and blocking it throughout the body could have unwanted effects.” The mice experienced no known side effects, but Gilroy says it may be safer to target EP2 in ageing macrophages specifically.

Finally, the team found similar patterns in human tissue, with liver samples from older people showing higher EP2 activity and more senescent neutrophils. “The human data are supportive, but still correlative,” says Gilroy. “We haven’t yet shown that blocking EP2 can restore neutrophil clearance in aged human tissue.”

The researchers are now planning to study whether this process affects the onset of conditions such as Alzheimer’s disease.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in aging research, demonstrating in mice that restoring the body's ability to clear senescent 'zombie' cells can improve cognitive and physical health outcomes. This has broad implications for human aging and age-related diseases, with promising preliminary human tissue data and potential for future therapies targeting conditions like Alzheimer's disease.
articles.personality_title: New drug helps clear harmful 'zombie' cells to improve aging in mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As we get older, certain cells called senescent or 'zombie' cells stop working properly and build up in our bodies. These cells release harmful substances that can damage other cells and make us weaker and less sharp.

**What happened** – Scientists at Stanford tested a drug on older mice to help their bodies remove these zombie cells. The drug blocks a signal called EP2 that stops immune cells from cleaning up the zombie cells. After treatment, the mice had fewer harmful cells, moved better, lost less muscle, and remembered things almost as well as young mice.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows a new way to fight aging problems by fixing the body's natural cleaning system instead of killing cells directly. The study also found similar patterns in human tissues, suggesting the drug might help people too, though more research is needed.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study if this treatment can slow down diseases like Alzheimer’s by helping the body clear these harmful cells in humans.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new drug that helps the body remove harmful aging cells improved memory and health in old mice, offering a hopeful path for future aging treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau médicament aide à éliminer les cellules 'zombies' pour mieux vieillir chez la souris
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, certaines cellules appelées sénescentes ou « zombies » cessent de fonctionner correctement et s'accumulent dans notre corps. Ces cellules libèrent des substances nocives qui peuvent endommager d'autres cellules et nous rendre plus faibles et moins vifs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques de Stanford ont testé un médicament sur des souris âgées pour aider leur corps à éliminer ces cellules zombies. Le médicament bloque un signal appelé EP2 qui empêche les cellules immunitaires de nettoyer ces cellules. Après le traitement, les souris avaient moins de cellules nuisibles, bougeaient mieux, perdaient moins de muscle et se souvenaient presque aussi bien que les jeunes souris.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre une nouvelle façon de lutter contre les problèmes liés à l’âge en réparant le système naturel de nettoyage du corps au lieu de tuer directement les cellules. L’étude a aussi trouvé des signes similaires dans des tissus humains, ce qui suggère que ce médicament pourrait aider les personnes aussi, même si des recherches supplémentaires sont nécessaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’étudier si ce traitement peut ralentir des maladies comme Alzheimer en aidant le corps à éliminer ces cellules nuisibles chez l’humain.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau médicament qui aide le corps à éliminer les cellules nuisibles liées au vieillissement a amélioré la mémoire et la santé chez des souris âgées, ouvrant une voie prometteuse pour de futurs traitements du vieillissement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo medicamento ayuda a eliminar células 'zombis' para mejorar el envejecimiento en ratones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Al envejecer, ciertas células llamadas senescentes o 'zombis' dejan de funcionar bien y se acumulan en nuestros cuerpos. Estas células liberan sustancias dañinas que pueden afectar a otras células y hacernos más débiles y menos ágiles.

Qué pasó** – Científicos de Stanford probaron un medicamento en ratones viejos para ayudar a sus cuerpos a eliminar estas células zombis. El medicamento bloquea una señal llamada EP2 que impide que las células inmunes limpien estas células. Después del tratamiento, los ratones tenían menos células dañinas, se movían mejor, perdían menos músculo y recordaban casi tan bien como los ratones jóvenes.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de combatir problemas de envejecimiento reparando el sistema natural de limpieza del cuerpo en lugar de matar las células directamente. El estudio también encontró patrones similares en tejidos humanos, lo que sugiere que el medicamento podría ayudar a las personas, aunque se necesita más investigación.

**Siguiente paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar si este tratamiento puede retrasar enfermedades como el Alzheimer ayudando al cuerpo a eliminar estas células dañinas en humanos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo medicamento que ayuda al cuerpo a eliminar células dañinas relacionadas con el envejecimiento mejoró la memoria y la salud en ratones viejos, abriendo un camino prometedor para futuros tratamientos contra el envejecimiento.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vigilant, glowing white blood cell figure gently guiding and clearing away shadowy, fading “zombie” cells within a softly illuminated, abstract representation of aging organs, symbolizing restored immune cleanup and renewed vitality.

articles.title: ‘Heat deaths are not inevitable’: WHO pushes Europe to heat‑proof its hospitals
articles.summary: As Europe battles a deadly record-breaking heatwave, the World Health Organization (WHO) issued new guidance on Thursday to help countries face scorching temperatures now and in the future.
articles.content:
Europe is the fastest-warming continent on the planet, with temperatures rising at approximately twice the global average, which is placing growing pressure on health systems, social care services and communities already facing more frequent and intense heatwaves.

Data from five countries already shows nearly 10,000 excess deaths linked to the extreme heat this summer.

The heat is on

WHO described extreme heat as an urgent and growing public health threat that is expected to hit harder each year. It is driven by climate change and worsened by urbanization and population ageing, rapidly increasing illness and deaths.

“Our region is the fastest-warming region globally. In the past four years alone, heat has claimed more than 200,000 lives, while heat-related mortality has risen by 30 per cent over the past 20 years,” said Dr Hans Kluge, WHO Regional Director for Europe.

He stressed that “heat deaths are not inevitable” because “we know how to protect people: warn communities early, make cities cooler, ensure access to water and shade, check on those most at risk and prepare health systems before temperatures peak.”

© WHO/Gilles Reboux

Hospitals feel the heat

The new guidance will help countries to strengthen Heat–Health Action Plans (HHAPs), which connect weather alerts to public health measures such as outreach to people most at risk, cooling spaces, surge planning in hospitals and clear advice for the public.

WHO underscored the need to build resilient health facilities. Heatwaves can overwhelm hospitals that may not be designed to cope with higher temperatures, especially as admissions rise.

This is particularly the case in cities, where built-up areas trap heat and remain warmer than surrounding rural or suburban areas.

Hospital buildings can overheat, causing power supplies, cooling systems as well as computers and tech services to fail. Moreover, staff as well as patients can become stressed and burned out due to the heat.

Romania’s ‘cool’ hospital

In Romania, Buhuși Hospital is adapting to meet the challenge.

The facility has established a designated cool space for people suffering from heatstroke, providing cool water and medical staff trained to spot the early signs of heat exhaustion. Authorities have also applied for funding to install a high-efficiency cooling system throughout the entire building.

Assessment, investment and readiness

WHO is supporting such efforts across Europe through its Hospital Safety Initiative which uses the Hospital Safety Index to assess whether health facilities are likely to remain operational during emergencies.

The index was traditionally used for hazards such as earthquakes and floods but is increasingly helping countries to identify climate and weather risks, including extreme temperatures.

Armenia, Georgia, Kazakhstan, Poland and Ukraine have conducted assessments and training with WHO support in recent years, helping authorities prioritise investments and improve readiness.

UK takes action

The United Kingdom provides another example of how Heat–Health Action Plans can guide response.

The UK’s Adverse Weather and Health Plan uses a colour-coded alert system, developed with the national Met Office, that links each alert level to practical actions for health services, local authorities, community groups and the public.

In May, officials issued amber alerts in anticipation of record-breaking temperatures of 35.1 degrees Celsius. Red alerts were issued the following month, when parts of the country reached unprecedented temperatures of up to 37.7°C.

New guidance

WHO first published comprehensive guidance on HHAPs in 2008.

The new guidance sets out eight core elements for countries and local authorities, namely governance, heat warning systems, protection for populations at increased risk, communication, health system resilience, reducing heat exposure, surveillance, and monitoring, evaluation and learning.

It also contains five user action briefs for key sectors and a public health message bank with practical steps people can take to protect themselves and others.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on WHO's new guidance to help European countries prepare health systems and hospitals for extreme heatwaves driven by climate change, aiming to reduce heat-related deaths and improve public health resilience. This has broad significance for public health across Europe, with concrete examples of adaptation and readiness efforts, providing a clear positive impact and actionable measures.
articles.personality_title: WHO urges Europe to make hospitals ready for extreme heat
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Europe is warming faster than any other continent, causing more heatwaves that put pressure on hospitals and health systems. This summer, five countries reported nearly 10,000 extra deaths linked to extreme heat.

**What happened** – The World Health Organization (WHO) released new advice to help European countries prepare for heatwaves. This includes improving warning systems, protecting vulnerable people, and making hospitals cooler and stronger against heat. For example, a hospital in Romania created a cool area for heatstroke patients and plans to install better cooling systems.

**Impact** – This guidance is important because heatwaves can overwhelm hospitals not built for high temperatures, causing equipment failures and stress for patients and staff. By following WHO’s advice, health facilities can better protect people and reduce deaths caused by extreme heat.

**What’s next step** – Countries are encouraged to use WHO’s new guidance to update their Heat–Health Action Plans. Some, like the UK, already have alert systems to warn the public and prepare health services. WHO will continue helping countries assess risks and improve hospital safety for heat emergencies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – WHO’s new guidance helps European hospitals prepare for rising heatwaves to save lives and keep health services running smoothly.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’OMS invite l’Europe à préparer ses hôpitaux aux fortes chaleurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Europe se réchauffe plus vite que tout autre continent, ce qui provoque davantage de vagues de chaleur mettant les hôpitaux et les systèmes de santé sous pression. Cet été, cinq pays ont signalé près de 10 000 décès supplémentaires liés à la chaleur extrême.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS) a publié de nouvelles recommandations pour aider les pays européens à se préparer aux vagues de chaleur. Cela inclut l’amélioration des systèmes d’alerte, la protection des personnes vulnérables et le renforcement des hôpitaux contre la chaleur. Par exemple, un hôpital en Roumanie a créé un espace frais pour les patients souffrant d’un coup de chaleur et prévoit d’installer un système de refroidissement plus performant.

**Impact** – Ces recommandations sont importantes car les vagues de chaleur peuvent submerger les hôpitaux non conçus pour de telles températures, provoquant des pannes d’équipement et du stress chez les patients et le personnel. En suivant les conseils de l’OMS, les établissements de santé peuvent mieux protéger les personnes et réduire les décès liés à la chaleur extrême.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays sont encouragés à utiliser les nouvelles recommandations de l’OMS pour mettre à jour leurs plans d’action contre la chaleur. Certains, comme le Royaume-Uni, disposent déjà de systèmes d’alerte pour prévenir le public et préparer les services de santé. L’OMS continuera d’aider les pays à évaluer les risques et à renforcer la sécurité des hôpitaux face aux urgences liées à la chaleur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les nouvelles recommandations de l’OMS aident les hôpitaux européens à se préparer aux vagues de chaleur croissantes afin de sauver des vies et d’assurer le bon fonctionnement des services de santé.
articles.personality_title_es: La OMS insta a Europa a preparar sus hospitales para el calor extremo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Europa se está calentando más rápido que cualquier otro continente, lo que provoca más olas de calor que ponen presión sobre hospitales y sistemas de salud. Este verano, cinco países reportaron casi 10,000 muertes extra vinculadas al calor extremo.

**Qué pasó** – La Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS) publicó nuevas recomendaciones para ayudar a los países europeos a prepararse para las olas de calor. Esto incluye mejorar los sistemas de alerta, proteger a las personas vulnerables y hacer que los hospitales sean más frescos y fuertes frente al calor. Por ejemplo, un hospital en Rumania creó un espacio fresco para pacientes con golpes de calor y planea instalar mejores sistemas de enfriamiento.

**Impacto** – Esta guía es importante porque las olas de calor pueden sobrecargar hospitales que no están diseñados para altas temperaturas, causando fallas en equipos y estrés en pacientes y personal. Siguiendo los consejos de la OMS, los centros de salud pueden proteger mejor a las personas y reducir muertes por calor extremo.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a los países a usar la nueva guía de la OMS para actualizar sus Planes de Acción para la Salud y el Calor. Algunos, como el Reino Unido, ya tienen sistemas de alerta para avisar al público y preparar los servicios de salud. La OMS seguirá apoyando a los países para evaluar riesgos y mejorar la seguridad hospitalaria ante emergencias por calor.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La nueva guía de la OMS ayuda a los hospitales europeos a prepararse para olas de calor más frecuentes y así salvar vidas y mantener los servicios de salud funcionando bien.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy hospital building surrounded by cooling blue mist and gentle water streams, with abstract silhouettes of medical staff tending to glowing, translucent human figures resting peacefully in shaded, cool spaces, set against a softly glowing European cityscape under a calm, warm-toned sky.
